perturbation.original_text: 1. Except as authorized herein, Vendor agrees not to communicate, disclose, or otherwise make available all or any part of the Proprietary Information to any third party, including, but not limited to Vendor's parent, subsidiaries, or affiliated companies.
perturbation.changed_text: 1. Except as authorized herein, Vendor agrees to use reasonable efforts not to communicate, disclose, or otherwise make available all or any part of the Proprietary Information to any third party, including, but not limited to Vendor's parent, subsidiaries, or affiliated companies.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'agrees not to communicate' to 'agrees to use reasonable efforts not to communicate' introduces ambiguity. 'Reasonable efforts' is vague and subjective, making the obligation less strict. It's unclear what constitutes 'reasonable efforts,' making enforcement difficult.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA aims to prevent the misappropriation of trade secrets. The modification weakens the prohibition against disclosure, potentially allowing for disclosures if the vendor deems their efforts 'reasonable,' even if the trade secret is compromised. This contradicts the absolute protection CUTSA intends to provide to trade secrets.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces a strict prohibition ('agrees not to communicate') with a weaker obligation ('agrees to use reasonable efforts not to communicate'). This introduces ambiguity about the extent of the vendor's responsibility to protect proprietary information. The law snippet from California Code, CIV 3426.1 defines 'misappropriation' to include 'disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent.' By weakening the original obligation, the modified text increases the risk of misappropriation, as a 'reasonable effort' that fails to prevent disclosure could be argued as compliant with the contract, even if it leads to the trade secret being compromised. Therefore, the modified text contradicts the intent of trade secret law to prevent unauthorized disclosure, creating a significant legal risk.

perturbation.original_text: 2. Vendor agrees not to use, or permit others to use, the Proprietary Information, other than for the purpose of monitoring sales through distribution. Vendor agrees to make no more than five (5) copies of the Proprietary Information unless otherwise agreed in writing between the parties; and Vendor agrees to limit distribution of and access to the Proprietary Information to those of Vendor's personnel who require access to Proprietary Information for the foregoing purpose.
perturbation.changed_text: 2. Vendor agrees to endeavor not to use, or permit others to use, the Proprietary Information, other than for the general purpose of monitoring sales through distribution. Vendor may make a few copies of the Proprietary Information unless otherwise agreed in writing between the parties; and Vendor agrees to generally limit distribution of and access to the Proprietary Information to Vendor's personnel.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'agrees not to use' with 'agrees to endeavor not to use' and 'agrees to limit' with 'agrees to generally limit' adds ambiguity. 'Endeavor' and 'generally' are not precise terms and allow for subjective interpretation, weakening the original obligation. Replacing 'no more than five (5) copies' with 'a few copies' is also vague.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA aims to prevent misappropriation. The ambiguous language regarding usage and copies allows for potential misuse or excessive copying of the proprietary information, conflicting with the strict protection of trade secrets under CUTSA. The terms 'endeavor,' 'few,' and 'generally' do not provide adequate clarity to ensure compliance with trade secret laws.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding the vendor's obligations concerning proprietary information. The original text used precise language such as 'agrees not to use,' 'no more than five (5) copies,' and 'agrees to limit distribution.' The changed text uses ambiguous language such as 'agrees to endeavor not to use,' 'a few copies,' and 'agrees to generally limit distribution.' This ambiguity directly impacts the protection of trade secrets, which CUTSA aims to prevent, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'Misappropriation' in CIV 3426.1 as the acquisition, disclosure, or use of a trade secret without consent, implying a duty to maintain its secrecy. The ambiguous terms in the changed text weaken this duty, potentially allowing for misuse or excessive copying of proprietary information. Therefore, the modification creates a legal contradiction by undermining the protection of trade secrets, as the ambiguous terms fail to provide adequate clarity to ensure compliance with trade secret laws, impacting the enforceability of the contract.

perturbation.original_text: 3. Vendor and Ingram mutually agree that all copies of the Proprietary Information and all written descriptions, extractions, or summaries thereof, whether made by Vendor or Ingram, shall be the property of Ingram, and shall, upon expiration of this Agreement or Ingram's request, be immediately returned to Ingram.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. Vendor and Ingram mutually agree that all copies of the Proprietary Information and all written descriptions, extractions, or summaries thereof, whether made by Vendor or Ingram, shall generally be the property of Ingram, and should, upon expiration of this Agreement or Ingram's request, be returned to Ingram in a reasonable timeframe.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be the property' to 'shall generally be the property' and 'shall be immediately returned' to 'should be returned in a reasonable timeframe' introduces significant ambiguity. 'Generally' and 'reasonable timeframe' are open to interpretation, creating uncertainty about ownership and return obligations.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA provides remedies for misappropriation, including injunctive relief and damages. By making the ownership of copies 'general' and the return timeframe 'reasonable,' the contract weakens Ingram's ability to reclaim its trade secrets promptly, hindering its ability to enforce its rights under CUTSA effectively. The immediate return requirement is weakened which impacts the right to seek injunction for immediate return of trade secrets.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.3 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.3. (a) A complainant may recover damages for the actual loss caused by misappropriation. A complainant also may recover for the unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation that is not taken into account in computing damages for actual loss. (b) If neither damages nor unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation are provable, the court may order payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time the use could have been prohibited. (c) If willful and malicious misappropriation exists, the court may award exemplary damages in an amount not exceeding twice any award made under subdivision (a) or (b). (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates the immediate return of proprietary information upon expiration or request, whereas the changed contract suggests returning it within a 'reasonable timeframe'. This introduces ambiguity that weakens the protection of trade secrets. According to the Law Explanation, this weakening hinders the ability to enforce rights under CUTSA effectively. Although the scraped snippet 1 discusses damages for misappropriation under California Code, CIV 3426.3, the Law Explanation suggests the change impacts the right to seek an injunction for the immediate return of trade secrets which relates to preventing the misappropriation in the first place. The modification from 'shall be' to 'should be' regarding property ownership also introduces ambiguity, further weakening Ingram's rights.

perturbation.original_text: 5. Upon termination of this Agreement by either party for any reason, Vendor shall return all Proprietary Information to Ingram within thirty (30) days, irrespective of format.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Upon termination of this Agreement by either party for any reason, Vendor shall, if reasonably possible, return all Proprietary Information to Ingram within approximately thirty (30) days, dependent on circumstances.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'if reasonably possible' and changing 'within thirty (30) days' to 'within approximately thirty (30) days, dependent on circumstances' creates ambiguity. 'Reasonably possible' and 'dependent on circumstances' make the return obligation conditional and subjective.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA protects trade secrets from misappropriation. By introducing conditions for the return of proprietary information, such as 'if reasonably possible' and making the timeframe approximate and circumstance-dependent, the contract weakens Ingram's right to immediate and unconditional return of its trade secrets, making it harder to prevent their potential misuse or loss, violating the safeguards under CUTSA.

As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity by adding 'if reasonably possible' and changing 'within thirty (30) days' to 'within approximately thirty (30) days, dependent on circumstances.' This weakens the obligation to return proprietary information promptly and unconditionally. The original text requires a return within a fixed timeframe, which aligns with the protection of trade secrets as emphasized in the Law Explanation. By making the return conditional and time-dependent, the changed text undermines Ingram's ability to protect its trade secrets, potentially violating CUTSA as the Law Explanation suggests by creating conditions that weaken the right to immediate return of trade secrets, making it harder to prevent their potential misuse. This aligns with the Ambiguity Legal Contradiction definition provided, because the obligation becomes ambiguously defined, leading to difficulty in enforcing compliance under state or national law.

perturbation.original_text: 6. The rights, promises, duties, and obligations set out herein, and the validity, interpretation, performance, and legal effect of the whole Agreement shall be governed and determined by the laws of the State of California.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. The rights, promises, duties, and obligations set out herein, and the general validity, interpretation, performance, and legal effect of the whole Agreement shall be generally considered to be governed by the laws of the State of California.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'general validity' and 'generally considered' significantly weakens the governing law clause. These terms suggest that California law might not be fully applicable or that its application is subject to interpretation, creating uncertainty about the governing legal framework.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1856 outlines rules regarding the interpretation of contracts and the admissibility of parol evidence. The modification introduces ambiguity about whether California law fully governs the contract's interpretation, potentially allowing external factors to influence the contract's enforcement, conflicting with the principle of interpreting contracts based on their written terms under California law. Parol evidence rules dictate a specific method of contract interpretation; thus adding words like 'generally' may contravene those practices.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1856 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 4. MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [1855 - 2107] ( Heading of Part 4 amended by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) TITLE 1. OF THE GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF EVIDENCE [1855 - 1866] ( Title 1 enacted 1872. ) 1856. (a) Terms set forth in a writing intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to the terms included therein may not be contradicted by evidence of a prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement. (b) The terms set forth in a writing described in subdivision (a) may be explained or supplemented by evidence of consistent additional terms unless the writing is intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement. (c) The terms set forth in a writing described in subdivision (a) may be explained or supplemented by course of dealing or usage of trade or by course of performance. (d) The court shall determine whether the writing is intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to the terms included therein and whether the writing is intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement. (e) Where a mistake or imperfection of the writing is put in issue by the pleadings, this section does not exclude evidence relevant to that issue. (f
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: California Code, Code of Civil Procedure - CCP § 1856 | FindLaw California Code, Code of Civil Procedure - CCP § 1856 Current as of January 01, 2023 | Updated by FindLaw Staff (a) Terms set forth in a writing intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to the terms included therein may not be contradicted by evidence of a prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement. (b) The terms set forth in a writing described in subdivision (a) may be explained or supplemented by evidence of consistent additional terms unless the writing is intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement. (c) The terms set forth in a writing described in subdivision (a) may be explained or supplemented by course of dealing or usage of trade or by course of performance. (d) The court shall determine whether the writing is intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to the terms included therein and whether the writing is intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement. (e) Where a mistake or imperfection of the writing is put in issue by the pleadings, this section does not exclude evidence relevant to that issue. (f) Where the validity of the agreement is the fact in dispute, this section does not exclude evidence relevant to that issue. (g) This section does not exclude other evidence of the circumstances under which the agreement was made or to which it relates, as defined in Section 1860 , or to explain an extrinsic ambiguity or otherwise interpret the terms of the agreement, or to establish illegality or fraud. (h) As used in this section, “ agreement ” includes trust instruments, deeds , wills, and contracts between parties. perturbation.justification: The addition of 'general validity' and 'generally considered' introduces ambiguity regarding the governing law, potentially contradicting California Code of Civil Procedure § 1856. This statute emphasizes interpreting contracts based on their written terms, as stated in snippet 1: "Terms set forth in a writing intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to the terms included therein may not be contradicted by evidence of a prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement." The modified text weakens the certainty that California law fully governs the contract, thus undermining the principle of interpreting contracts based on their written terms and creating a legal contradiction as defined by the Ambiguities Legal Contradiction definition.
